text: Use broad-spectrum SPF 30+; reapply every 2 hours outdoors. Seek shade midday; wear hat and sunglasses. Severe blistering burns or fever may need medical care.
Drink water regularly; increase fluids with heat, exercise, or illness. Signs of serious dehydration include dizziness, very dark urine, or confusion — seek care if severe.
With prolonged vomiting or diarrhea, oral rehydration solutions can replace salts better than water alone. See a clinician if you cannot keep fluids down or feel faint.
Heat stroke is an emergency: confusion, hot dry skin, high body temperature. Call emergency services; move to shade, cool gradually. Prevention: hydration, breaks, and avoiding peak heat.
Move to a cool place, loosen clothing, sip cool water. If vomiting, confusion, or symptoms worsen, seek urgent care — it can progress to heat stroke.
Warm gradually with dry layers and warm drinks if alert. Severe confusion, slurred speech, or stopped shivering are emergencies — call for help and avoid rapid rewarming extremes.
Warm affected areas gradually with body heat or lukewarm water; do not rub ice. Numb white or gray skin after cold needs medical evaluation.
Remove ticks with fine-tipped tweezers close to skin; clean the site. Watch for expanding rash or fever — contact a clinician about testing and prevention in endemic areas.
Remove stinger if present; clean site; cold compress. Call emergency services for throat swelling, wheezing, or widespread hives (possible anaphylaxis).
Keep calm, immobilize limb loosely, and seek emergency care immediately. Do not cut, suck, or apply ice. Note snake appearance if safe to describe.
Wash skin and clothing after exposure. Calamine or OTC creams may help itching. Seek care for widespread rash, face/genitals, or breathing difficulty.
Cool with running water 10–20 minutes; do not apply ice directly. Cover with clean nonstick dressing. Seek care for large, deep, or facial/hand burns.
Apply firm pressure with clean cloth; elevate if possible. Seek emergency care if bleeding does not slow, wound is deep, or caused by animal bite.
Lean forward slightly, pinch soft part of nose 10–15 minutes. Avoid blowing after. Frequent or heavy nosebleeds warrant medical evaluation.
Rest, ice 15–20 minutes, compression wrap, elevation. Seek care if you cannot bear weight, bone tenderness, or severe deformity.
Immobilize, ice, and seek urgent evaluation for deformity, crunching sound, numbness, or inability to use the limb.
Rest physically and cognitively; avoid another head hit. Red flags: worsening headache, repeated vomiting, confusion, seizure — emergency evaluation.
Hydration is key. Seek care for high fever, blood in stool, severe dehydration, or symptoms beyond 72 hours.
Pregnant people should avoid unpasteurized dairy and undercooked deli foods per public health guidance; ask your clinician about food safety lists.
Folic acid before and early pregnancy supports neural tube development; iron and vitamin D needs vary — follow your prenatal care team's advice.
Early frequent feeding supports supply; seek lactation support for pain, poor weight gain, or mastitis symptoms.
Mood changes after delivery are common; persistent sadness, guilt, or intrusive thoughts need prompt clinician support — treatments help.
Discuss bothersome symptoms with a clinician; options range from lifestyle changes to medications — risks and benefits are individualized.
Screening decisions depend on age, risk, and preferences — discuss pros and cons with a clinician rather than self-interpreting tests.
Monthly self-exams are not universally recommended, but report new lumps, pain, or heaviness promptly — many causes are treatable when caught early.
Report new persistent lumps, skin changes, or nipple discharge to a clinician. Screening mammography schedules depend on age and risk.
Cervical screening reduces cancer risk; intervals depend on age and past results — follow your national guidelines and clinician advice.
Weight-bearing exercise, adequate calcium/vitamin D, and fall prevention matter. Screening and treatment decisions are individualized after menopause or with risk factors.
Any fever in infants under 3 months needs urgent evaluation. Older children: hydration and comfort; seek care for lethargy, rash, breathing issues, or prolonged fever.
Pain control and sometimes observation or antibiotics depending on age and severity — follow your pediatric clinician's plan.
Rapid testing or culture guides antibiotics when indicated. Finish prescribed antibiotics; return if worsening or neck swelling.
Many cases are viral; saline rinses and pain relief help. See a clinician if symptoms >10 days, high fever, or severe facial pain.
Avoid touching eyes; wash hands; do not share towels. Bacterial vs viral management differs — seek care for pain, vision changes, or contact lens wearers.
See a clinician for diagnosis and antibiotics when bacterial UTI is confirmed. Cranberry products are not a substitute for treatment.
Severe colicky pain, blood in urine, or inability to urinate needs urgent care. Prevention includes hydration and sometimes diet changes per specialist advice.
Acute attacks need medical management; long-term control includes diet discussion and medications — follow rheumatology or primary care guidance.
Smaller meals, weight management, and avoiding late eating help. Persistent symptoms or swallowing issues need evaluation.
Track triggers; fiber and stress management help some people. Red flags like blood in stool or weight loss need medical evaluation.
Celiac is an autoimmune condition diagnosed with testing — lifelong gluten-free diet is essential; do not self-diagnose by diet alone.
Many tolerate small amounts or lactose-free products. Persistent GI symptoms should be evaluated for other causes.
Travel and risk-based recommendations vary — ask your clinician about schedules and boosters.
HPV vaccination reduces certain cancers; schedules differ by age — discuss with a clinician.
Recommended for most adults 50+; ask about contraindications and timing after prior chickenpox or shingles.
RSV can be serious in infants and older adults; preventive options depend on age and season — follow public health and clinician guidance.
Recommended for certain ages and college/military/travel situations — verify with your provider.
See a travel clinic 4–6 weeks before trips for vaccines and malaria prophylaxis if needed.
Shift sleep gradually before travel; seek morning light at destination; limit alcohol; melatonin may help some — ask your clinician.
Face forward, fresh air, and OTC options can help; persistent vomiting risks dehydration — seek care if severe.
Ascend gradually, hydrate, and descend if severe headache or confusion — altitude illness can be dangerous.
Pull over to rest; caffeine is not a substitute for sleep. Chronic daytime sleepiness needs evaluation (e.g., sleep apnea).
Loud snoring with daytime sleepiness warrants evaluation. Treatment improves heart and metabolic risks when used consistently.
Discuss iron stores and medications with a clinician; lifestyle tweaks sometimes help symptoms.
Thyroid disorders affect energy, weight, and heart rate — diagnosis requires blood tests; do not adjust hormones without medical guidance.
Fatigue and pallor are clues; causes range from diet to bleeding — evaluation finds the source before self-supplementing iron.
Sun exposure and diet contribute; supplementation should follow lab and clinician advice, especially in osteoporosis risk.
Deficiency can mimic neurologic and blood problems — diagnosis and replacement dosing need medical oversight.
Use ABCDE changes as a prompt to see a dermatologist; sunscreen reduces risk but does not eliminate it.
Start age and interval depend on risk and guidelines — discuss screening modality with your clinician.
Annual LDCT is for defined high-risk smokers/ex-smokers — ask if you qualify.
Smoking cessation is the most effective treatment; inhalers and pulmonary rehab improve quality of life — follow a lung specialist plan.
Emphasize vegetables, whole grains, lean proteins, and healthy fats; limit ultra-processed foods and excess sodium.
Hidden sodium is common in packaged foods; label reading helps control blood pressure alongside other lifestyle measures.
Cardiovascular risk combines numbers with blood pressure, diabetes, and smoking — treatment is personalized; do not stop statins without medical advice.
Can increase stroke risk; anticoagulation decisions use scoring tools — managed by cardiology or primary care.
Daily weights and fluid/sodium limits may be advised; worsening shortness of breath or leg swelling needs prompt contact with your care team.
New asymmetric swelling could be a clot — urgent evaluation. Bilateral swelling has many causes including heart, liver, kidney, and medications.
Leg pain/swelling after travel, surgery, or immobility needs urgent assessment; prevention includes movement and prescribed anticoagulation when indicated.
Sudden pleuritic chest pain and breathlessness are emergencies — call emergency services.
Fever with confusion, fast breathing, or low blood pressure after infection is an emergency — seek immediate care.
Good wound care and completing antibiotics when prescribed help resistance; recurrent infections need medical guidance.
Can follow antibiotics; hygiene matters. Severe diarrhea with fever needs prompt medical care.
Take antibiotics exactly as prescribed; never share leftovers. Many illnesses are viral and do not need antibiotics.
Wash 20+ seconds with soap; critical before eating and after bathroom — reduces respiratory and GI spread.
Monitor at eye level, neutral wrists, and frequent breaks reduce strain; persistent numbness needs evaluation.
Most acute low back pain improves in weeks with activity as tolerated; red flags include leg weakness, numbness, or bladder issues — urgent care.
Symptoms often improve with time and guided exercises; severe weakness or bowel/bladder changes are emergencies.
Weight management, physical therapy, and low-impact exercise help; injections or surgery are individualized.
Early treatment reduces joint damage — diagnosis and DMARDs require rheumatology.
Multimodal care (sleep, exercise, CBT, medications) helps; diagnosis excludes other conditions.
Pacing, sleep care, and specialist support matter; diagnosis requires careful evaluation for other causes.
Trauma-focused therapies (e.g., PE, EMDR) are effective; crisis lines exist if safety is a concern — seek licensed care.
Medical monitoring is essential for electrolytes and heart risks — multidisciplinary treatment saves lives.
Diagnosis is clinical; treatments include behavioral strategies and medications when appropriate — managed by qualified clinicians.
Support needs vary; early interventions focus on communication and skills — seek credible developmental evaluations.
Safety planning, driving discussions, and caregiver support matter; sudden confusion needs urgent evaluation for reversible causes.
Strength/balance training, vision checks, and home hazard reduction reduce fractures — discuss bone health after falls.
Sudden painless vision loss or curtain-like symptoms are emergencies — call immediately.
Gradual loss warrants audiology; sudden hearing loss is urgent — seek same-day care.
Brush twice daily with fluoride, floss, regular dental visits; untreated decay can spread infection.
Bleeding gums warrant dental care; links to heart and diabetes risk make oral health part of whole-person care.
Non-healing sores, white/red patches, or neck lumps need dental or ENT evaluation — especially with tobacco/alcohol use.
Many infections are asymptomatic; regular testing based on risk and barrier methods reduce spread — confidential care is available.
PrEP and PEP are effective when used correctly — require clinician prescribing and monitoring.
Curable oral regimens exist — screening is recommended for certain birth cohorts and risk factors.
Testing is risk-based; treatment of latent TB prevents future illness — follow public health protocols.
Antimalarial choice depends on destination and resistance patterns — travel clinic essential.
Prevention is bite avoidance; pregnant travelers should review destination advisories with clinicians.
Early antibiotics for confirmed or high-likelihood cases; not every tick transmits — follow evidence-based protocols.
Limit outdoor exertion when AQI is poor; N95 helps some; sensitive groups should be extra cautious.
Install detectors; symptoms like headache and nausea in winter with heaters warrant fresh air and emergency services if suspected.
Constant supervision of children, barriers around pools, and life jackets near open water save lives.
Helmets reduce brain injury risk; replace after a significant impact.
Safety planning matters; hotlines can help with local resources — if in immediate danger, call emergency services.
In the US, call or text 988 for mental health crisis support; if someone is in immediate danger, call emergency services.
Community naloxone programs save lives; after reversal, emergency evaluation is still needed.
Combining counseling with medications improves quit rates — ask clinicians about FDA-approved options.
Not harmless; youth should not vape. Adults switching from cigarettes should discuss risks with clinicians.
Guidelines vary by country; avoid alcohol in pregnancy; seek help if drinking interferes with life — treatment works.
Moderate caffeine is generally safe for most adults; excess can worsen anxiety, sleep, and palpitations.
Not appropriate for everyone (e.g., diabetes on insulin, eating disorders); discuss with a clinician before major changes.
Can affect lipids and medications; medical supervision is important for therapeutic ketosis in epilepsy — general weight loss should be clinician-guided.
Plan protein, B12, iron, and calcium sources; balanced plant-based patterns can support heart health.
Prehydrate; replace fluids for long endurance events; watch for hyponatremia from over-drinking plain water in extreme endurance — follow sports medicine advice.
Gradual training increases, strength work, and proper footwear help; persistent pain needs evaluation.
Reduce training load, check footwear; stress fractures must be ruled out if focal bone pain persists.
Stretching, supportive shoes, and night splints help many; prolonged symptoms need podiatry.
Control blood pressure and diabetes; avoid nephrotoxic NSAID overuse; follow nephrology labs.
Weight loss and limiting alcohol improve many cases; monitoring follows risk factors.
Typical pain after fatty meals; complications like fever or jaundice need urgent care.
Severe epigastric pain radiating to back with vomiting is an emergency — alcohol and gallstones are common causes.
Worsening RLQ pain, fever, or loss of appetite needs urgent evaluation — do not delay.
Flank pain and fever with UTI symptoms need prompt treatment — can become serious.
Chronic pelvic pain warrants gynecologic evaluation; treatments range from hormones to surgery.
Metabolic and fertility aspects vary — individualized care with primary or specialty clinicians.
Headache, vision changes, upper abdominal pain, or sudden swelling in pregnancy need urgent obstetric evaluation.
Follow pediatric thresholds for feeding and bilirubin; severe jaundice needs timely care to prevent complications.
Back to sleep, firm flat surface, no soft bedding; avoid overheating — follow updated safe sleep guidelines.
Use stage-appropriate seats; rear-facing as long as limits allow; replace seats after moderate/severe crashes per manufacturer.
Children in older housing should follow screening guidelines; developmental concerns need evaluation.
Radon is a leading lung cancer cause in non-smokers in some regions — test your home and mitigate if high.
Walking/cycling when safe improves fitness and local air quality — personal and population benefits.
Mindfulness and structured breaks can reduce burnout; persistent impairment may need therapy.
Social connection affects mental and physical health — community programs and tele-friends can help.
Financial and mental health harms are real; confidential helplines and therapy exist.
Consistent family media plans and sleep protection matter; problematic use may need professional support.
Light therapy, CBT, and sometimes medications help — discuss diagnosis with a clinician.
Pets can support routine and companionship but are not a substitute for medical or psychiatric care.
Bring specific questions to a clinician or pharmacist; credible sources include national immunization technical advisories.
Trials advance medicine; eligibility and informed consent protect participants — discuss with study teams.
Ask for teach-back, written instructions, and interpreters when needed — better understanding improves outcomes.
Reasonable for major diagnoses or surgery; bring records and specific questions.
Keeping an updated medication list and allergy list speeds urgent care and reduces errors.
Pharmacists can review interactions, inhaler technique, and vaccination schedules — accessible experts.
Useful for many follow-ups; emergencies and exams sometimes still need in-person care.
ER for life threats; urgent care for non-emergency issues when PCP unavailable — when unsure, nurse lines can guide.
Federally qualified health centers and community clinics provide primary care on income-based fees in many regions.
Ask for itemized bills; charity care and payment plans may exist — patient advocates can help navigate appeals.
Documenting wishes reduces family stress; review periodically and share copies.
Registration reflects your wishes; discuss with family so they can support decisions.
Eligibility criteria protect donor and recipient health — hydration and iron matter; deferrals are common and temporary.
Know how to use auto-injectors; carry two doses; still call emergency services after use.
Applied tension technique and exposure strategies help; tell vaccinators — they are used to it.
Not only end-of-life — focuses on quality of life alongside treatment; ask for consults early when appropriate.
Comfort-focused care when goals shift — teams support patients and families emotionally and medically.
Grief is non-linear; counseling and support groups help; seek help if functioning is severely impaired or suicidal thoughts appear.
